coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical study of PCM thermophysical parameters on lithium-ion battery thermal management
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: SI:Energy Storage - driving towards a clean energy future
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Rui
Li, Zhi
Hong, Wenhua
Wu, Qichao
Yu, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion battery (LIB) nowadays plays a key role as one of the most widely used energy storage technologies such as the application in electric vehicles. Thermal management critically affects the performance of LIB. Phase change cooling using phase change material (PCM) has become a promising method due to its high latent heat and no need to consume extra pump power. In this study, thermal performance of a battery module with 25-parallel 18650 lithium-ion battery has been numerically and experimentally investigated by adopting phase change cooling with PCM. Firstly, the internal resistance of the unit cell has been experimentally tested to build up the heat generation model. Based on the heat generation model and heat transfer model, the effects of PCMs thermophysical parameters including thermal conductivity, latent heat and porosity are investigated when they are applied in the thermal management of designed LIB module. The results indicate that the higher thermal conductivity and latent heat lead to better thermal management performance comprehensively considering the maximum temperature and temperature uniformity of LIB module. Results also illustrate that thermal management performance increases very slightly when the thermal conductivity and latent heat attain a relatively high value. As for the porosity of paraffin/metal foam, an optimal value of 94% exists to achieve the lowest maximum temperature for LIB module.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery thermal management
Phase change material
Energy storage
Lithium-ion battery
Temperature uniformity

Effects of pure paraffin with expanded graphene
Effects of thermal conductivity of PCM
Effects of latent heat of PCM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical model of LIB pack with 25 18650 cell unit: (a) before packed by PCM; (b) after packed by PCM.
The test rig of the unit cell: (a) The connection of test circuit; (b) the image of the test device.
The pictures of paraffin and expanded graphene: (a) Paraffin; (b) Expanded graphene.
The preparation process of composite PCM.
The preparation process of composite PCM.
The schematic diagram of monitoring points. (a) normal condition; (b) stressed condition.
Temperature field of LIB cooled by PCM under general condition: (a) paraffin; (b) paraffin/EG.
Temperature field of LIB cooled by PCM under stressed condition: (a) Paraffin; (b) Paraffin/EG.
Temperature field and liquid volume fraction of LIB central surface at the end of the discharging process under normal condition.
Temperature field and liquid volume fraction of LIB central surface at the end of discharging process under stressed condition.
Average surface temperature of LIB cooled by PCMs with different thermal conductivity under normal condition.
Maximum temperature of LIB pack cooled by PCMs with different thermal conductivity.
Temperature field of LIB pack cooled by PCMs with different thermal conductivity.
The maximum temperature and temperature difference of LIB cooled by PCM with different latent heat.
The temperature field and liquid PCM distribution battery module under extreme condition.
Maximum temperature of LIB using pure paraffin, paraffin/EG and paraffin/metal foam.
The effects of porosity on the maximum temperature and temperature difference.
The Ohm resistance of unit cell under different temperature and SOC.
The polarization resistance of unit cell under different temperature and SOC.
The value of parameters in Eqs. (10) and (11).
Thermophysical parameters for the paraffin and composite PCM.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical study of PCM thermophysical parameters on lithium-ion battery thermal management

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Energy Engineering, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery (LIB) nowadays plays a key role as one of the most widely used energy storage technologies such as the application in electric vehicles. Thermal management critically affects the performance of LIB. Phase change cooling using phase change material (PCM) has become a promising method due to its high latent heat and no need to consume extra pump power. In this study, thermal performance of a battery module with 25-parallel 18650 lithium-ion battery has been numerically and experimentally investigated by adopting phase change cooling with PCM. Firstly, the internal resistance of the unit cell has been experimentally tested to build up the heat generation model. Based on the heat generation model and heat transfer model, the effects of PCMs thermophysical parameters including thermal conductivity, latent heat and porosity are investigated when they are applied in the thermal management of designed LIB module. The results indicate that the higher thermal conductivity and latent heat lead to better thermal management performance comprehensively considering the maximum temperature and temperature uniformity of LIB module. Results also illustrate that thermal management performance increases very slightly when the thermal conductivity and latent heat attain a relatively high value. As for the porosity of paraffin/metal foam, an optimal value of 94% exists to achieve the lowest maximum temperature for LIB module.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most promising technologies replacing the Internal Combustion Engine in transport sectors, electric vehicles are currently widely developed in the markets (Dua et al., 2019). Lithium-ion battery (LIB) is one of the most important power sources for electric vehicles due to its high energy storage density, long cyclic lifespan and low self-discharge (Ouyang et al., 2018; Fathabadi, 2014). The performance of LIB is sensitive to the temperature since the optimal working temperature range is narrow to achieve high charging–discharging​ efficiency. LIB is resistive to generate extra heat in the working process after long time utilization (Hémery et al., 2014; Sabbah et al., 2008). The harsh temperature variations of LIB during charging and discharging process may result in safety issue causing serious fire accidents. LIB thermal management is crucial to maintain the operational conditions within the optimal working temperature range to avoid thermal runaway (Huang et al., 2019). For conventional cooling methods such as air cooling (Chen et al., 2019), water cooling (Xu et al., 2019) and thermoelectric cooling (Lyu et al., 2019), these methods need to consume additional power and electricity posing potential pressure to the limited capacity of vehicle batteries. In recent years, thermal management of LIB based on phase change cooling using phase change material (PCM) has been attracting increasing attention, owing to its superiority in temperature control performance, simple layout and without additional power consumption (Li et al., 2018; Kizilel et al., 2008).

Hallaj and Selman first proposed the idea of using PCM for LIB thermal management (Hallaj and Selman, 2000; Al-Hallaj and Selman, 2002). Results indicated that the maximum temperature of the LIB pack using phase change cooling is 8 °C lower than that of natural air cooling. Goli et al. (2014) prepared graphene-enhanced composite PCM for thermal management of LIB considering the low thermal conductivity of paraffin. It was observed that the combination of graphene and PCM led to a significant reduction in the temperature rise of the LIB module compared to the conventional pure paraffin. Wu et al. (2016) conducted experimental studies on battery thermal management using copper mesh-enhanced paraffin with expanded graphene. The results showed that the developed novel composite PCM presented quite better performance of heat dissipation and temperature uniformity, especially in stressed conditions. Zhang et al. (2018) designed a battery thermal management system using PCM embedded with kaolin and expanded graphene. The effects of the fraction of additives on the temperature control performance were analysed and the most suitable composite PCM with 10 wt% expanded graphene and 10 wt% kaolin was selected. In the thermal management tests, the maximum temperature of a unit cell could be effectively controlled within 45 °C and the temperature difference between unit cells was restrained within 5 °C even at a high discharge rate. Moreover, some studies are related to the effects of PCM thermal conductivity on the thermal performance of LIB under cold environment. For example, Ghadbeigi et al. (2018) compared the warming up performance of LIB using pure paraffin and commercial PCM module with graphite/paraffin composites under short and long stop conditions. The results indicated that pure paraffin with low thermal conductivity provided a slight advantage against PCM composites by keeping the LIB module warm and improving heat retention. Based on the above-reviewed studies, it can be concluded that the thermal conductivity of PCM has a great influence on the battery thermal management performance and the combination of PCM and additives is the main thermal conductivity enhancement method, but there is lack of information in how much extent the thermal conductivity should be enhanced. In addition, the effects of other parameters of pure PCM such as latent heat and composite PCM such as porosity are not detailed revealed. For example, the latent heat determines the response performance of PCM.

Other researchers have concentrated on different layouts of PCMs,combination of PCM and other active cooling methods. Because the PCM-based battery thermal management system still undergoes limited heat capacity if the heat absorbed by PCM cannot be efficiently dissipated into the environment (Kim et al., 2019). Ramandi et al. (2011) proposed the combination of two different PCM for LIB thermal management. The effects of utilizing one and two PCM were compared when the battery discharged with constant rate at the ambient temperature of 30 °C. The results indicated that the two-PCM layout was slightly more efficient than one-PCM layout. Javani et al. (2014) investigated the impact of different PCM thickness on the LIB thermal management when the battery charged and discharged at different power density at the ambient temperature of 21 °C. Ling et al. (2015) compared the LIB thermal management performance of using only PCM system and system that combines PCM and forced air cooling. As a result, the maximum temperature of battery pack using composite system could be controlled within 50 °C while it exceeded 60 °C for only PCM system in two cycles. Zhao et al. (2017) experimentally studied the coupled LIB thermal management using heat pipes to release the heat absorbed by PCM. The results showed that the coupled thermal management system combining PCM and heat pipes could maintain the maximum temperature of battery pack under 50 °C for longer time than that of air-based and PCM-based LIB thermal management systems. Jiang and Qu (2019) developed a sandwich structure composing of battery pack, PCM and heat pipes. Different impactors including melting temperature of PCM, heat transfer coefficient of heat pipes in the condensation and thickness of PCM were investigated.

In summary, very limited researches have paid attention to the effects of thermophysical parameters such as the thermal conductivity and latent heat of PCM, as well as the porosity of composite PCM, which need to be further revealed to understand how much extent these thermophysical properties affect the thermal performance of LIB for the better selection of proper PCM in the future. In this study, at first the internal resistance of the unit cell is experimentally tested in order to obtain heat generation rate of unit cell. Then the effects of PCMs thermophysical parameters including thermal conductivity and latent heat are investigated when they are applied in the thermal management of designed LIB module. Finally, the effects of porosity for PCM/metal foam are studied. The results of this study can provide a deeper insight into selecting optimal PCM and corresponding thermos-physical parameters, as well as the layouts of PCM-based battery thermal management.

A battery pack with twenty-five 18650 Li-ion cell units is studied, which is shown in Fig. 1. The cell units are in aligned layout and packed by PCM. The space between the adjacent cell unit is 10 mm. Therefore, the mathematical model includes two parts: PCM and LIB. For PCM, the enthalpy method is applied to solve the heat transfer and phase change process.
In order to simplify the physical problem, some assumptions need to be made as follows Javani et al. (2014), Rao et al. (2011):
(1) Heat transfer inside the liquid PCM is dominated by conduction.
(2) The average specific heat capacity for cell unit and PCM in applied the simulation.
(3) The cell unit has orthotropic thermal conductivity.
(4) The initial temperature of battery pack is the same as the ambient temperature.
(5) The natural convection in the liquid PCM is negligible.
(6) The radiation in the heat transfer process is neglected.

Based on the previous assumptions, the energy equation for the PCM is expressed as (Qin et al., 2019):

In order to produce the heat generation model, the internal resistance of unit cell has been experimentally tested. Besides, the composite PCM of paraffin/expanded graphene is prepared and its thermophysical properties are tested considering the low thermal conductivity of pure paraffin.

The heat generation of the unit cell is described in Eq. (7). From the equation, it can be known that the heat generation rate is related to the current and resistance, which should be determined by experimental test. The resistance of the unit cell consists of the Ohm resistance and polarization resistance, which rely on the temperature and SOC of the unit cell. The layout of the test rig is shown in Fig. 2.

The objective of this experiment is to obtain the correlation of internal resistance with the temperature and SOC to calculate the heat generation rate of the unit cell. In the experiments, at first the unit cell that has been fully charged is placed in the thermotank, then the Ohm resistance and polarization resistance are measured under different constant ambient temperature and SOC in the discharging process. The experimental results of Ohm resistance and polarization resistance are shown in Tables 1 and 2, respectively.

According to the experimental results, the data is fitted in the MATLAB 2018b. a correlation of internal resistance with the temperature and SOC is obtained by a quartic curve fitting with the smallest error. The correlation is described by the following equations:
(11)
R
O

The detailed value of parameters in Eqs. (10) and (11) are listed in Table 3. In the following simulation, the heat generation rate is set as the source term in the ANSYS/Fluent software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and test of composite PCM
ce:section.ce:para: In order to enhance the thermal conductivity of pure paraffin, the composite PCM of paraffin and expanded graphene is prepared considering the low thermal conductivity. The pictures of paraffin and expanded graphene are shown in Fig. 3. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 4.

After completing the preparation of composite PCM, the thermophysical parameters should be tested for the later numerical simulation. Based on the differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) technique and other instruments such as LFA, the thermophysical properties including thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, latent heat and melting temperature for pure paraffin and composite PCM are systematically tested. The DSC curve for composite PCM is shown in Fig. 5 the detailed parameters are listed in Table 4.

The main aim of this study is to evaluate the effects of PCM thermos-physical parameters on the thermal management performance of LIB. Firstly, the thermal management performance using pure paraffin and paraffin/expanded graphene are compared considering the low thermal conductivity of pure paraffin at normal and stressed operating conditions. Then the detailed effects of thermal conductivity are analysed for the better selection of potential PCM in future research. Later, other parameters including latent heat of paraffin and porosity of composite PCM are analysed. Because the LIB may discharge at a different rate, the thermal management performance of LIB under normal and stressed operating conditions are analysed. The discharging rate and ambient temperature are 3C, 25 °C and 5C, 35 °C for normal operating condition and stressed operating condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pure paraffin with expanded graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the low thermal conductivity of pure paraffin, the comparison of paraffin and paraffin/expanded graphene (EG) is conducted. The thermophysical parameters for the paraffin and composite PCM are listed in Table 4. The thermal management performance of LIB pack using paraffin and composite PCM under different conditions are compared. The temperature at different points (point 2, 8 and 13) are monitored to evaluate the thermal management performance. The place of the monitoring points is shown in Fig. 1.

Fig. 6 shows the temperature evolution of different monitoring points when the LIB pack finishes the discharging process under normal condition and stressed condition. It can be known that the temperature of LIB cooled by composite PCM almost maintains unchanged under normal condition at the end of the discharging process. The maximum temperature among these three monitoring points is 46.5 °C. As for paraffin cooling, the maximum temperature of LIB increases much slower after 600 s than paraffin/EG cooling. And the maximum temperature attains 50.2 °C, which exceeds the optimal working temperature range. In regard to the thermal performance using pure paraffin and paraffin/EG under stressed conditions, the PCM starts to melt at about 100 s due to the high heat generation rate under stressed condition. Compared to the paraffin cooling, the temperature of LIBs using paraffin/EG cooling increases with a much smaller rate and is slower in the overall discharging process. The maximum temperature of paraffin/EG cooling is 49.2 °C while it is 53.4 °C.

Figs. 7 and 8 show the overall temperature field of LIB packs cooled by paraffin and paraffin/EG at the end of the discharging process under normal condition and stressed condition. It is obvious that the temperature field of LIB cooled by paraffin/EG is much more uniform than that of pure paraffin under both normal and stressed condition. In order to have a deeper insight into the difference of thermal management performance, the temperature field and liquid volume fraction of the X–Y​ surface at the end of discharging process are presented in Figs. 9 and 10 for the two cases.

In Fig. 9(a), it can be seen that the temperature difference between the LIB at the central place and the paraffin near the wall are quite high (more than 12 °C ) for the case of paraffin cooling, while it is only 6 °C for the case of paraffin/EG cooling. The low thermal conductivity of pure paraffin leads to that the heat generated by LIBs cannot be easily transferred from the centre to the peripheral paraffin, and the heat accumulates in the centre of LIB pack. In contrast, due to the high thermal conductivity of paraffin/EG, the heat generated by LIBs can be delivered to the surrounding paraffin and then to the ambient.

It can also be seen that adopting paraffin/EG achieves better temperature control performance than pure paraffin from the distribution of liquid volume fraction at the end discharging process shown in Fig. 10. For the case of paraffin cooling, only paraffin near the LIB unit cell melts because the heat generated by LIB cannot be conducted to the outer paraffin very fast. For the paraffin/EG cooling, paraffin at the centre and outer melts due to the high thermal conductivity of composite PCM whether it is normal condition or stressed condition. Although pure paraffin has larger latent heat than paraffin/EG, the high thermal resistance led by low thermal conductivity during the heat transfer process makes the latent heat cannot be utilized well. Overall, the thermal conductivity of PCM used for LIB thermal management is a crucial parameter, which deserves further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of thermal conductivity of PCM
ce:section.ce:para: In Section 4.1 it has been proved that thermal conductivity has a great influence on the thermal LIB management performance using PCM cooling. However, how large the thermal conductivity of PCM should need to be further studied. Besides, adding expanded graphene can improve the thermal conductivity of composite PCM, but it also decreases the latent heat and density. Therefore, in order to find the optimal range of thermal conductivity, the effects of thermal conductivity on the LIB thermal management performance are analysed in this section. In the simulation, some hypothetical PCMs with different thermal conductivity are analysed under normal condition and stressed condition.

Fig. 11 shows the average surface temperature of LIBs changes with discharging time using PCM cooling with different thermal conductivity under normal condition. With the thermal conductivity increasing from 0.1 W/(m K) to 10 W/(m K), the average surface temperature of LIB increases with a smaller rate. It​ can be observed that the average surface temperature of LIB decreases with the rise of PCM thermal conductivity at any moment during the discharging process of normal condition. When the PCM thermal conductivity attains 3 W/(m K), the average surface temperature decreases very limitedly compared with the case of 1 W/(m K), which means the thermal conductivity is large enough to maintain good heat dissipation efficiency of LIB module under normal condition. When​ the PCM continues to increase to 10 W/(m K), it is reasonable to see the slight difference of average surface temperature between the case of 3 W/(m K) and 10 W/(m K) as shown in Fig. 11.

Fig. 12 shows the maximum temperature of LIB pack under different thermal conductivity at the moment of finishing the discharging process. It can be seen that the maximum temperature decreases by 6.3 °C when the thermal conductivity increases from 0.1 W/(m K) to 15 W/(m K). Especially in the range of 0.1 ∼ 3 W/(m K), the maximum temperature decreases very fast. But when the thermal conductivity increases from 3 W/(m K) to 15 W/(m K), the cooling effects are not significant. When the thermal conductivity attains 3 W/(m K), the maximum temperature of LIB pack can be well controlled by 45 °C and 48 °C for normal condition and stressed condition at the end of discharge. The results illustrate that the thermal conductivity of PCM is not as big as possible. When the thermal conductivity exceeds a certain range, the cooling effects increases very slightly. For example, when the thermal conductivity of PCM increases from 5 W/(m K) to 15 W/(m K), the maximum temperature of LIB pack only reduces 0.31 °C and 0.66 °C for normal condition and stressed condition, respectively.

The temperature field of LIB cooled by PCM with different thermal conductivity shown in Fig. 13 can obviously reflect the effects of thermal conductivity on thermal management performance. When the thermal conductivity is large enough, increasing the thermal conductivity almost have little effects on the temperature field of LIB pack. It can be concluded that the low thermal conductivity leads to bad thermal management performance. Thermal conductivity enhancement is an important method to improve the LIB thermal management performance of phase change cooling. But the thermal conductivity is not as large as possible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of latent heat of PCM
ce:section.ce:para: Latent heat of PCM is an important parameter to affect the LIB thermal management performance. In order to investigate the effects of latent heat and find optimal latent heat of PCM, the thermal management performance of some hypothetical PCMs with different latent heat are analysed. The latent heat changes from 125 J/g to 275 J/g with a constant difference of 25 J/g. All the hypothetical PCMs has a thermal conductivity of 3 W/(m K). Fig. 14 shows the maximum temperature and temperature difference of LIB cooled by PCMs with different latent heat under normal condition and stressed condition. It can be seen that the maximum temperature of LIB keeps decreasing with the increase of latent heat in both the normal condition and stressed condition, but the decreasing rate of the maximum temperature is more significant for stressed condition comparing with that of the normal condition. In detail, when the latent heat increases from 125 J/g to 275 J/g, the maximum temperature of LIB decreases from 47.8 °C to 46.5 °C under normal condition while it decreases from 54 °C to 49.4 °C under stressed condition. The trend of temperature difference is similar to the maximum temperature under normal condition and stressed condition. The temperature difference declines from 1.2 °C to 0.6 °C under normal condition while it reduces from 2.9 °C to 0.9 °C with the latent heat of PCM rising from 125 J/g to 275 J/g. As is known to us all, the heat generation rate is much higher under stressed condition than that of normal condition, the results indicate that the PCM with large latent heat is effective to reduce the maximum temperature and maintain small temperature difference for stressed condition, due to the larger latent heat can absorb more heat generated by the LIBs. But for normal condition, the latent heat need not be as big as possible because of the limited heat generation rate of LIB. Therefore, the operating conditions especially the stressed condition of LIB module should be considered when selecting PCMs with proper latent heat and designing the PCM-based thermal management system.

It can also be seen that the maximum temperature difference LIBs first decreases rapidly and then with a small rate with the improvements of latent heat in stressed condition. The maximum temperature difference is 2.92 °C when the latent heat of phase change is 125 J/g, and the maximum temperature difference of the battery is 0.95 °C at 275 J/g. But in normal condition, the maximum temperature difference of LIBs decreases gradually with the increases of latent heat in the whole range. The results indicate that the selection of latent heat should be comprehensively considered based on the normal condition and stressed condition. Fig. 15 shows the temperature field of X–Y surface at the end of discharging process when the latent heat is 125 J/g and 275 J/g under stressed condition. It is apparent the large latent heat makes lower average temperature and more uniform temperature field. However, in the practical application, the latent heat is not as big as possible, because the larger latent heat always means the lower thermal conductivity. In this study, the optimal latent heat is 225 J/g for the designed LIB pack considering the temperature control performance.

Presently, the metal foam is always added into the pure paraffin to improve the heat transfer performance due to its ultra-high thermal conductivity. When the metal foam is embedded in the paraffin, the paraffin will fill the pore of metal foam, forming the paraffin/metal foam composite PCM. The porosity of metal foam is a crucial parameter that affects the heat transfer process. Therefore, in this section, the effects of metal foam porosity are investigated. Fig. 16 compares thermal management performance of LIB using pure paraffin, paraffin/EG and paraffin/metal foam. It can be found that the paraffin/metal foam achieves the best temperature control performance among these three PCMs, because the high thermal conductivity enhances the heat transfer process from LIB to the surrounding PCM and from central PCM to the outer PCM, resulting in slower temperature increase and more uniform temperature field. In detail, the maximum temperature of LIBs using paraffin/metal foam is 6.7 °C lower than using pure paraffin.

Because of the importance of porosity of metal foam, the thermal management performance of LIBs cooled by paraffin/metal foam with different porosity is analysed, and the results of the maximum temperature and temperature difference are shown in Fig. 17. When the porosity of metal foam is smaller than 90%, the maximum temperature declines rapidly with the increase of porosity of metal foam. When the porosity of metal foam is larger than 90%, the maximum temperature first decreases and then increases with the rise of porosity, but there is no much difference between each porosity. The porosity of 94% achieves the lowest maximum temperature of 44.5 °C. As for the temperature difference, although the temperature difference of LIBs increases with the rise of metal foam porosity, in each case the paraffin/metal foam can control the temperature difference of LIBs within 1 °C, indicating the superiority of metal foam in temperature control performance. In this study, the optimal metal foam porosity can be selected as 94% comprehensively considering the maximum temperature and temperature difference.

In this study, the effects of thermophysical parameters of PCMs are investigated in detailed, due to the knowledge gap in understanding how these parameters influence the LIB thermal management performance using PCM cooling and selecting potential PCMs in the future real applications. Based on the experimental test and simulative study, some conclusions are drawn as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Paraffin/EG achieves better thermal management performance than the pure paraffin in both normal condition and stressed condition for LIB pack, the reason is the high thermal conductivity of paraffin/EG can easily conduct the heat generated by the LIBs to the surrounding PCM and from the centre to the outer PCM, as a result, the maximum temperature is reduced and the temperature field is more uniform.

The thermal conductivity is not as big as possible for the thermal management of LIB module since the cooling effects increase very slightly when the thermal conductivity exceeds a certain range. For example, in the range of 0.1 ∼ 3 W/(m K), the maximum temperature decreases very fast, but when the thermal conductivity of PCM increases from 5 W/(m K) to 15 W/(m K), the maximum temperature only reduces 0.31 °C and 0.66 °C for normal and stressed condition, respectively.

Results show that the larger latent heat achieves smaller maximum temperature and temperature difference for LIB pack, especially in a stressed condition. However, in practical application, the latent heat is not as big as possible, because the larger latent heat always means the lower thermal conductivity. In this study, the optimal latent heat is 225 J/g for the designed LIB pack considering the temperature control performance.

The application of paraffin/metal foam can control the temperature difference of LIB pack within 1 °C at any porosity due to the ultrahigh thermal conductivity, but the maximum temperature of LIB pack first decreases rapidly and then increases gradually with the rise of porosity of metal foam. The optimal porosity of 94% exists to succeed best thermal management performance comprehensively considering the maximum temperature and temperature difference of LIB pack.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the significant effects of thermal conductivity and latent heat of PCM, as well as the properties of additive (graphene and metal foam), the future working plan will be implemented from following aspects: (1) finding the most proper PCMs for the objective battery system by numerical and experimental study, which have the optimal phase change temperature, latent heat of phase change and thermal expansion. (2) improving the thermal conductivity of PCM by working on the properties of the metal/carbon foam (density and porosity) or the expanded graphite (mass fraction and density).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has received supports from the following three funds the Young Scholar Cultivation Program of Zhejiang Science and Technology Association No. 2018YCGC015, University Laboratory Research Program of Zhejiang Province No. ZD201906 and Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province No. LQ20E060008, Grant title: Fundamental Research on Power Battery Thermal Reliability Management in All Working Conditions. The authors also would like to thank the finical support from UK-China Joint Research and Innovation Partnership fund from Newton Fund under the grant number 201703780098 and the grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant number No. 51976176, No. 51806189.
